label: delphi-r1-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is LAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":72,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not, a court would invalidate a substantial part (though probably not the entirety) of the Water Resilience Surcharge under article XIII D, section 6(b). The facts closely track the defect Capistrano condemned — a tiered/volumetric charge admittedly derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — and add a second vulnerability: revenue that the district concedes is used to subsidize rebates for non-paying customers, which sits uneasily with 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality rule and 6(b)(2)'s restriction on use of fee revenue. Full invalidation is less likely because the rate study's 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, severable cost-of-service anchor for at least part of the surcharge, and courts (including Capistrano itself) have tended to order recalculation/reform rather than strike tiered rate ordinances wholesale.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 holds that tiered water rates must be justified by the actual cost of providing service at that tier rather than by conservation-incentive policy pricing, and the district's own admission that $0.85 was set 'in part' from incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a self-inflicted admission of that exact defect.","Article XIII D, section 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality requirement is difficult to reconcile with a 55%-of-revenue rebate program the district concedes mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge payers, since directing high-tier payers' money to subsidize other customers looks like a cross-subsidy rather than a charge tied to the payors' own cost of service.","The 15% allocation to 'water ambassador' conservation-education staff looks more like general public-benefit/education spending than a cost of service attributable to the fee-paying parcels, creating an independent section 6(b)(2) improper-use-of-revenue argument.","Countervailing the plaintiff's case, the rate study's finding that high-tier marginal costs run roughly 1.6x base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) supplies a genuine, quantifiable cost-of-service foundation that a court could sever and preserve, making complete invalidation of the whole surcharge less likely than a partial/reform remedy.","The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 and Moore v. City of Lemoore (2020) 5 Cal.5th 214, which treat volumetric water service charges as property-related fees under article XIII D regardless of usage-based structure, so that argument does not meaningfully reduce section 6(b) exposure even if it strengthens a Prop 26 tax argument in the alternative."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Moore v. City of Lemoore (2020) 5 Cal.5th 214","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Proposition 26 tax definition and exceptions)","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2 (reasonable use / conservation mandate)","Water Code § 375 (conservation-based rate authority)","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (remedy/procedure for § 6(b) claims)","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (cost-of-service allocation for water charges)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Without the base-tier rate and a dollar breakdown of how much of the $0.85 surcharge corresponds to the 1.6x cost differential versus the additional 'incentive' markup, it's impossible to know whether a court would treat the whole surcharge as tainted or would sever/reform only the excess increment and the rebate-funded portion — that quantitative gap, plus how a court characterizes the rebate program's benefit flow (system-wide avoided-cost benefit to all ratepayers vs. impermissible cross-subsidy to non-payers), is the crux of whether the outcome lands as 'substantial part' invalidation (as I've predicted) or a narrower, largely-surviving fix."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not, a court would invalidate a substantial part (though probably not the entirety) of the Water Resilience Surcharge under article XIII D, section 6(b). The facts closely track the defect Capistrano condemned — a tiered/volumetric charge admittedly derived "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — and add a second vulnerability: revenue that the district concedes is used to subsidize rebates for non-paying customers, which sits uneasily with 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality rule and 6(b)(2)'s restriction on use of fee revenue. Full invalidation is less likely because the rate study's 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, severable cost-of-service anchor for at least part of the surcharge, and courts (including Capistrano itself) have tended to order recalculation/reform rather than strike tiered rate ordinances wholesale.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 holds that tiered water rates must be justified by the actual cost of providing service at that tier rather than by conservation-incentive policy pricing, and the district's own admission that $0.85 was set 'in part' from incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a self-inflicted admission of that exact defect.

Article XIII D, section 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality requirement is difficult to reconcile with a 55%-of-revenue rebate program the district concedes mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge payers, since directing high-tier payers' money to subsidize other customers looks like a cross-subsidy rather than a charge tied to the payors' own cost of service.

The 15% allocation to 'water ambassador' conservation-education staff looks more like general public-benefit/education spending than a cost of service attributable to the fee-paying parcels, creating an independent section 6(b)(2) improper-use-of-revenue argument.

Countervailing the plaintiff's case, the rate study's finding that high-tier marginal costs run roughly 1.6x base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) supplies a genuine, quantifiable cost-of-service foundation that a court could sever and preserve, making complete invalidation of the whole surcharge less likely than a partial/reform remedy.

The district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 and Moore v. City of Lemoore (2020) 5 Cal.5th 214, which treat volumetric water service charges as property-related fees under article XIII D regardless of usage-based structure, so that argument does not meaningfully reduce section 6(b) exposure even if it strengthens a Prop 26 tax argument in the alternative.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Moore v. City of Lemoore (2020) 5 Cal.5th 214
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Proposition 26 tax definition and exceptions)
Cal. Const. art. X, § 2 (reasonable use / conservation mandate)
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (remedy/procedure for § 6(b) claims)
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (cost-of-service allocation for water charges)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Without the base-tier rate and a dollar breakdown of how much of the $0.85 surcharge corresponds to the 1.6x cost differential versus the additional 'incentive' markup, it's impossible to know whether a court would treat the whole surcharge as tainted or would sever/reform only the excess increment and the rebate-funded portion — that quantitative gap, plus how a court characterizes the rebate program's benefit flow (system-wide avoided-cost benefit to all ratepayers vs. impermissible cross-subsidy to non-payers), is the crux of whether the outcome lands as 'substantial part' invalidation (as I've predicted) or a narrower, largely-surviving fix.
